name: Money — Rupees, Paise and Making Amounts
whatIsIt: Money comes in coins and notes with different values (₹1, ₹2, ₹5, ₹10, ₹20…). To MAKE an amount we add up coins and notes. To find CHANGE we subtract what we paid minus the cost. ₹1 = 100 paise, so money works just like place value — bigger notes are like the tens and hundreds.
whyNeeded: Buying anything — a pencil, a snack, a bus ticket — means adding up money and checking your change. Handling money confidently is one of the most useful everyday maths skills there is.
realLifeUses: Paying for a snack and counting the change
Checking a shopkeeper gave the right change
Saving coins in a piggy bank and totalling them

vocabulary.meaning: The main unit of Indian money; ₹1 = 100 paise.
The total value of coins and notes together.

story.title: Ishaan Buys a Pencil
story.text: Ishaan wants a pencil that costs ₹7. He has a ₹10 note. 'How will you make sure the change is right?' asks the shopkeeper. Ishaan thinks: 'I gave ₹10, the pencil is ₹7, so change is 10 − 7 = ₹3.' The shopkeeper hands back a ₹2 coin and a ₹1 coin. 'Two plus one is three — correct!' smiles Ishaan.
story.extractedProblem: Ishaan pays ₹10 for a ₹7 pencil. How much change should he get?

visual.caption: ₹10 + ₹5 + ₹2 = ₹17. Add coins and notes to make an amount.

teachingGallery.title: Make amounts and find change
teachingGallery.note: Add notes/coins to make an amount; subtract for change.

standardMethod.summary: To make an amount, add the values of the notes and coins. To find change, subtract the cost from what you paid.
steps: List the notes and coins you have or need.
To MAKE an amount: add their values (₹10 + ₹5 + ₹2 = ₹17).
To find CHANGE: change = amount paid − cost.
Check: cost + change should equal what you paid.

workedExamples.problem: Riya buys a book for ₹35 and pays with a ₹50 note. What change should she get?

steps: Start with the biggest note that fits: ₹20.

commonMistakes.mistake: Adding rupees and paise as if they were the same, e.g. treating 50 paise as ₹50.
commonMistakes.fix: Keep rupees and paise separate. ₹1 = 100 paise, so 50 paise is half a rupee, not ₹50.

commonMistakes.mistake: Adding the cost to the money paid instead of subtracting to find change.
commonMistakes.fix: Change is what's LEFT: change = paid − cost. Adding gives a wrong, bigger number.

medium.q: A snack costs ₹18. You pay ₹25. How much change (in ₹)?

challenge.q: A toy costs ₹64. You pay with a ₹100 note. What change (in ₹)?
Meena has 50 paise. She says 'I have ₹50!' What's wrong?

options.label: 50 paise is half a rupee, not ₹50
Nothing — paise and rupees are the same

challenge.answer: 50 paise is half a rupee, not ₹50
hintLadder: 50 paise is less than one rupee.

questions.q: A pen costs ₹12. You pay ₹20. Change in ₹?
You paid ₹50 for a ₹42 item. To check the change is right, cost + change should equal…

teachBackPrompt: Set up a pretend shop with a few items and price tags. 'Sell' something to a grown-up, take their money, and explain out loud how you worked out the exact change.

revisionCard.summary: Make an amount by ADDING notes and coins (₹10+₹5+₹2=₹17). Find change by SUBTRACTING: change = paid − cost. ₹1 = 100 paise, so keep rupees and paise separate. Check: cost + change = paid.

rememberIt.hook: Rupees and paise: add the same columns, count change UP
rememberIt.unpack: 100 paise = 1 rupee. Line up decimal points. Change = count up from cost to cash given.

studentQuestions.a: Count UP from the price to the money paid. Cost 65, paid 100: 65->70->100 is 35.

studentQuestions.q: How do I add amounts of money?
studentQuestions.a: Line up the decimal points and add like decimals - paise under paise, rupees under rupees.
